topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the accounting department seemed to hum with malicious intent that Tuesday morning. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers mocking me with their sheer disparity. Sarah, the new analyst, had just presented her quarterly projections, and the final compensation breakdown was… staggering. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and slowly, deliberately, I began to tap a rhythm on the desk with my pen. Each tap was sharp, a tiny, controlled explosion of frustration bubbling just beneath my skin. I wanted to shout, to point out the blatant imbalance, but the words caught in my throat, lodging like grit. I just shook my head, a slow, heavy motion, and picked up my coffee mug, the lukewarm liquid suddenly tasting like ash.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the sound barely audible over the low drone of the office HVAC. I looked over at Mark, who was currently basking in the glow of his latest performance review, a smug little smirk playing on his lips. My hands clenched into fists beneath the desk, the knuckles turning white. It wasn't just the salary difference; it was the sheer audacity of it, the way he seemed to glide through the system while I was stuck wrestling with spreadsheets that seemed designed by a sadist. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing loudly in the otherwise quiet corner of the bullpen. I walked over to the water cooler, gripping the cold metal fixture until my fingers ached, trying to regulate the sudden, hot surge of irritation flooding my chest.

[story 3]

I reread the email three times. Three times. The figures were undeniable, stark and unforgiving. My eyes burned as I tried to process the reality that someone who consistently delivered subpar work was being compensated significantly more than me. A low, involuntary sigh escaped my lips, a sound heavy with suppressed annoyance. I started pacing the narrow aisle between cubicles, my footsteps quick and uneven. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. My shoulders slumped momentarily, a heavy weight settling on my back, before I straightened up again, a grim set to my mouth. I felt a distinct need to reorganize my entire workflow, to find some way to channel this churning feeling into something productive, even if it just meant staring at the monitor until my eyes blurred.
